did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a non-standard activation function for melanoma classification. The study achieved 81.53% accuracy and implemented the model in a mobile application using the Flutter framework.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel CNN architecture with a non-standard activation function, training and validation on a specific dataset (900 training, 379 test images), and the implementation of a mobile application using Flutter. It reports new results, including an accuracy of 81.53%, and is published in the proceedings of the 2024 IEEE International Conference on Smart Information Systems and Technologies.

title: Mobile Application of Convolutional Neural Networks for Melanoma Classification
journal: 2024 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Information Systems and Technologies (SIST)
brief_summary: This study addresses the need for accessible melanoma screening by developing a mobile application powered by a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a non-standard activation function. The model was trained and validated using the ISIC 2016 dataset, which comprised 900 training images and 379 test images of benign and malignant skin lesions. To enhance model generalization, the researchers employed image augmentation techniques, including random rotation, random flipping, and resizing. The primary evaluation metric reported was accuracy, alongside other standard metrics such as recall, specificity, and precision. The proposed CNN model achieved an accuracy of 81.53% on the test set. Finally, the software was implemented as a user-friendly mobile application using the Flutter framework to facilitate early detection and proactive healthcare practices.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study specifically focused on melanoma skin cancer. The experiment used the ISIC 2016 dataset, where the 'malignant' class consisted of 173 images in the training set and 75 images in the test set.
The study differentiated between benign and malignant melanomas. The 'benign' class in the ISIC 2016 dataset consisted of 727 images in the training set and 304 images in the test set.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'benign' and 'malignant' (melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'Melanoma Classification'. The model outputs an array with 2 values representing the probabilities of each class (benign vs malignant), with the larger value chosen as the result. The study uses the ISIC 2016 dataset with two classes: benign (727 training, 304 test images) and malignant (173 training, 75 test images). The primary metric reported is accuracy (81.53%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016 Dataset
datasets_used.description: The 'Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection' challenge dataset from the ISIC Archive, containing annotated skin lesion images (dermoscopic and macroscopic) of malignant and benign lesions.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model with image augmentation (random rotation, random flip, resize).
use.testing: Used for testing the CNN model to evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, recall, specificity, precision).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy (81.53%), recall (6.7%), specificity (100%), precision (100%), and F-score (0.126) on a test set.

ISIC 2016 Dataset.notes: The model achieved 81.53% accuracy. The study notes low recall (6.7%) and high specificity (100%), attributing the low recall to significant class imbalance in the dataset. The model was implemented using PyTorch and deployed in a mobile application using Flutter.

architecture_details.activation_function: Non-standard activation function defined as y(x) = β tanh(softplus(-x)), where β = x*exp(x), proposed to overcome vanishing gradient problems compared to tanh, sigmoid, and ReLU
architecture_details.output_structure: Array with 2 values representing probabilities of each class (benign vs malignant)

training_details.validation_schedule: Validation check performed after each 5th epoch

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: The larger of the two output values is chosen as the result

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with non-standard activation function
preprocessing_techniques: Image augmentation: Random Rotation (rotating images by a randomly generated angle at each epoch)
Image augmentation: Random Flip (horizontal and vertical flips applied with a specific probability)
Image augmentation: Resize (resizing images to a specified dimension to ensure uniform input size for the CNN)
Normalization (rescaling pixel values to have a mean of 0 and variance of 1, resulting in a range of [-1; 1])

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated by a randomly generated angle at every epoch to imitate variability in smartphone camera angles. Applied 'on-the-fly' during the data-loading process.
Both horizontal and vertical flips were applied. A specific probability determined whether the image would be flipped before each epoch. Applied 'on-the-fly' during the data-loading process.
Images were resized to a specified dimension to ensure uniform input size for the CNN, as CNNs require fixed input sizes. Applied 'on-the-fly' during the data-loading process.

Pixel values were rescaled to have a mean of 0 and a variance of 1, resulting in a range of [-1; 1]. This was achieved by subtracting the mean value from the pixel value and dividing by the standard deviation.

conclusions: The proposed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a non-standard activation function achieved an accuracy of 81.53% on the ISIC 2016 dataset for melanoma classification.
The model demonstrated high specificity (100%) and precision (100%), but low recall (6.7%) and F-score (0.126).
The low recall and high error rate (18.47%) were attributed to significant class imbalance in the dataset, where benign lesions vastly outnumbered malignant ones.
Image augmentation techniques (random rotation, random flip, resize, and normalization) contributed to improved model generalization and robustness.
The study successfully implemented the trained CNN model into a user-friendly mobile application using the Flutter framework to facilitate accessible melanoma screening.

Future improvements require addressing class imbalance, enlarging the dataset capacity (including smartphone-camera photos), and adjusting hyperparameters such as learning rate, batch size, and the number of layers to better balance precision and recall.
important_remarks: The model exhibits critically low recall (6.7%) and a high false negative rate (70 out of 75 malignant cases missed), indicating a significant risk of failing to detect melanoma in clinical use.
The study is limited by severe class imbalance in the ISIC 2016 dataset (727 benign vs. 173 malignant training images), which the authors acknowledge as a primary cause of poor recall.
The dataset is small (900 training, 379 test images), limiting the statistical power and generalizability of the results.

The model was trained and tested exclusively on the ISIC 2016 dataset; there is no external validation on independent datasets or real-world smartphone-captured images, despite the app's intended use case.
Key hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of epochs) are not reported, preventing reproducibility of the training process.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it impossible to assess the variance or robustness of the results.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing or significance analysis was performed.

The mobile application was implemented using the Flutter framework, but the paper does not address potential data leakage risks between the training data and any potential user-generated data in the app.

The study acknowledges that existing AI-based mobile apps show high variability in performance (sensitivity 7%-73%, specificity 37%-94%), highlighting the difficulty of achieving reliable clinical performance with smartphone images.
The authors note that current mobile apps often provide false reassurance to users with cancerous lesions, a risk this model's low recall exacerbates.
